column1: What is Persistence as a tactic (TA0003)?
column2: A tactic used to maintain access to a compromised system across reboots and other interruptions

column1: How do persistence techniques generally work?
column2: They make configuration changes that re-execute a payload on a pre-defined trigger or event

column1: Why is persistence particularly important for adversaries?
column2: Because they may not be able to reliably regain a foothold via their initial access technique after losing it

column1: What are the two privilege contexts under which persistence can be added?
column2: Standard user (userland) or local admin (elevated)

column1: What is Boot or Logon Autostart Execution (T1547)?
column2: A collection of techniques where an adversary configures the computer to automatically execute a payload during startup or when a user logs in

column1: What are the two common user-level Registry Run Keys used for persistence?
column2: HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run and HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\RunOnce

column1: What is the difference between the Run key and the RunOnce key?
column2: The Run key is persistent and remains across every reboot; the RunOnce key is automatically deleted after its first execution

column1: How is the Registry Run Key leveraged for persistence?
column2: By writing a new registry value containing the path to a program to run when the user logs in

column1: What is the Startup Folder and how is it used for persistence?
column2: A special folder at %APPDATA%\Microsoft\Windows\Start Menu\Programs\Startup where any executable placed will automatically run each time the user logs in

column1: What registry key contains user environment variables and can be abused for logon script persistence?
column2: HKCU\Environment

column1: What is the UserInitMprLogonScript value and how is it used?
column2: A value added to the HKCU\Environment key that contains a path to a program, causing it to execute automatically when the user logs in (T1037.001)

column1: What is a PowerShell Profile (profile.ps1)?
column2: A script that executes automatically when new PowerShell windows are opened by a user

column1: Why do developers legitimately use PowerShell profiles?
column2: To customise the appearance and behaviour of their PowerShell sessions

column1: How can an adversary abuse PowerShell profiles for persistence?
column2: By creating or modifying a user's profile.ps1 to execute malicious code when PowerShell is opened (T1546.013)

column1: Where is the default PowerShell profile location for a user?
column2: $HOME\Documents\WindowsPowerShell\Profile.ps1

column1: What is an important constraint when placing code in a PowerShell profile for persistence?
column2: The code must not block execution, because the user will not receive an input prompt until the profile script finishes

column1: How can a blocking payload in a PowerShell profile be worked around?
column2: By executing the payload via the Start-Job cmdlet, which runs it as a background job

column1: What triggers can Windows Scheduled Tasks use?
column2: A specific time on a daily/weekly/monthly schedule, when the computer goes idle, system startup, user logon, or when a system event occurs

column1: What format are Windows Scheduled Tasks defined in at their core?
column2: XML format

column1: What MITRE technique ID covers Scheduled Task persistence?
What is the Component Object Model (COM)?
column2: An interoperability standard that allows applications written in different languages to reuse the same software libraries

column1: What is a COM interface?
column2: A software contract that defines the methods a caller can invoke, without exposing the internal workings of the library

column1: What is a COM object (component)?
column2: An interface and its associated implementation — the actual working code behind the interface

column1: What is a CLSID?
column2: A unique identifier (GUID) used to track each COM object in the registry, found under HKEY_CLASSES_ROOT\CLSID

column1: What registry keys under a CLSID entry point to the COM object's implementation on disk?
column2: InProcServer32 (pointing to a DLL) or LocalServer32 (pointing to an EXE)

column1: What is COM Hijacking (T1546.015)?
column2: A technique where an adversary changes or leverages a COM registry entry to trick an application into loading their malicious code instead of the intended COM object

column1: What are the two main ways COM hijacking can be achieved?
column2: By writing a HKCU entry for a CLSID that only exists in HKLM (missing HKCU entry), or by exploiting a COM entry that points to a DLL/EXE that no longer exists on disk but is in a writable location

column1: How does HKCU take priority in COM resolution for standard user processes?
column2: For processes running as a standard user, COM entries in HKCU take priority over entries in HKLM

column1: Why might a COM entry point to a non-existent file on disk?
column2: When third-party software is uninstalled and fails to remove the COM registry entries it added during installation

column1: What two properties make an ideal COM object to hijack?
column2: It should not break software or Windows when hijacked, and it should not be loaded so frequently that it renders the system inoperable

column1: What tool is commonly used to find COM hijacking opportunities?
column2: Process Monitor (procmon) from Sysinternals, which displays real-time registry access

column1: What filters should be applied in procmon to find COM hijacking opportunities?
column2: Filter for Operation = RegOpenKey, Path contains InprocServer32 or LocalServer32, and Result = NAME NOT FOUND

column1: Why is exporting procmon results useful when hunting for COM hijacks?
column2: It enables frequency analysis to find COM objects that are loaded a modest number of times, avoiding objects loaded too frequently

column1: Where is analysis and testing of a COM hijack ideally performed before using it on a target?
column2: On the adversary's own machine (e.g. a VM replicating the target environment) to ensure stability

column1: What does a ThreadingModel value need to be set to when creating a COM hijack entry?
column2: It should be set to match an appropriate threading model (e.g. "Both" or "Apartment") so the COM object loads correctly
